metadata.seed: Oh, there's a couple things. My classwork has been really been bogging me down lately, and then while I'm still doing better at work, I'm still not where I want to be. I don't want to have to consciously think this is why I'm. I want to keep working on. And I'm not being so argumentative at work. And then my boyfriend has been, like I said earlier, really gritting on my nerves, and I don't, like, I told him the other day that I wanted to break up with him, but he told me he was going to change and that things would get better. So I'm kind of torn with whether or not to continue in their relationship or just let it go. And at the same time, I'm having a hard time keeping up with my schoolwork and getting everything done on time. And my homework isn't where I want it to be. Like, I feel like I can do better.

patient.query: Oh, there's a couple things. My classwork has been really been bogging me down lately, and then while I'm still doing better at work, I'm still not where I want to be. I don't want to have to consciously think this is why I'm. I want to keep working on. And I'm not being so argumentative at work. And then my boyfriend has been, like I said earlier, really gritting on my nerves, and I don't, like, I told him the other day that I wanted to break up with him, but he told me he was going to change and that things would get better. So I'm kind of torn with whether or not to continue in their relationship or just let it go. And at the same time, I'm having a hard time keeping up with my schoolwork and getting everything done on time. And my homework isn't where I want it to be. Like, I feel like I can do better.

automatic_thoughts: I'm not where I want to be
I want to break up with him
Things will get better
I can do better
behaviors: struggling to keep up with homework

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re juggling a lot of pressure—from schoolwork and work performance to a relationship that’s leaving you feeling torn. What do you think would be most important for you to feel more in control, whether that’s focusing on your studies, your job, or your personal relationship? How might you weigh the potential benefits and challenges of each option to help clarify what feels right for you right now?

patient.query: I’ve been feeling really overwhelmed by school lately. It’s like no matter how hard I try, I just can’t seem to keep up. I’m constantly worried about disappointing my family if I don’t do well. It’s exhausting.

automatic_thoughts: I can't keep up
I'm disappointing my family
I'm not trying hard enough

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of pressure and feeling exhausted. What evidence do you have that you can’t keep up, and what might be some ways you could show your family that you’re doing your best?

patient.query: Yeah, it does feel like a lot. I guess I just look at my grades and the stuff I’ve turned in, and it just doesn’t feel like enough. I mean, I could talk to my family about it, but I’m afraid they won’t understand, or worse, that they'll think I’m not trying hard enough. It’s just really hard to open up.

automatic_thoughts: it doesn't feel like enough
they won't understand
they'll think I'm not trying hard enough
it's hard to open up

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy worry about how your grades and work are seen, and that makes it hard to open up to your family. Feeling like you’re not meeting expectations can feel isolating, especially when you fear they’ll judge you. It’s understandable that you’re hesitant to share these thoughts.

patient.query: Yeah, it really is isolating. I just wish I could feel... I don’t know, more confident or sure of myself when it comes to school. But every time I sit down to study, I just end up feeling paralyzed, like I'm not really making any progress. It’s like a never-ending loop of anxiety and frustration.

automatic_thoughts: I'm not really making any progress
I wish I could feel more confident

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling stuck and unsure of how to move forward. What evidence do you have that you’re not making progress when you study? Are there moments when you’ve felt a bit more confident or noticed any small steps forward?

patient.query: I guess there are times when I understand a concept better, but then it feels like it just slips away. So I don’t know if it really counts. I just keep comparing myself to others, and they seem to get it so easily. It makes me feel... less capable, I suppose. I don’t really want to feel that way, but it’s hard not to.

automatic_thoughts: it just slips away
I don’t know if it really counts
they seem to get it so easily
I feel less capable

neutral: Finding of self perception, self concept pattern (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling uncertain about your grasp of new ideas, especially when you see others picking them up quickly. What does it feel like when you think you’ve understood something? Have you noticed any moments where you can recall or apply it later?

patient.query: When I think I’ve understood something, there’s this brief moment of relief, like I can finally breathe. But then... I start to doubt it, like maybe it was just a fluke. And yeah, there are moments when I can apply it, but they feel pretty rare. More often, I’m just stuck in my head, worrying about whether I really know what I’m doing or if I’m just fooling myself. It’s frustrating.

automatic_thoughts: maybe it was just a fluke
I’m just fooling myself

llm_response.response: It sounds like you get a quick sense of relief when you think you’ve grasped something, only to have that feeling waver. What clues or experiences make you feel that it might have been just a fluke? If a close friend were in your shoes, what would you tell them about the evidence that supports or challenges that doubt?

patient.query: That's exactly it. I can feel good for a moment, but then I start picking apart everything I did. I think back to times I struggled with similar things and it just feels like I’m bound to mess up again. If it were a friend, I’d probably tell them to focus on the times they did well, but... I don’t really do that for myself. I guess I’m just too hard on myself.

automatic_thoughts: I start picking apart everything I did
I’m bound to mess up again
I don’t really do that for myself

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response:
It sounds like you notice a pattern of self‑criticism after a brief moment of feeling good.  
What evidence do you have that you will mess up again?  
If you were talking to a friend in that situation, what would you say to them?

patient.query: Yeah, I definitely see that pattern. As for messing up again, I just remember past experiences when things didn’t go well, and it feels like a guarantee that it’ll happen again. If I were talking to a friend, I’d probably tell them that everyone has ups and downs and that one mistake doesn’t define their whole journey. But saying that to myself feels completely different… it’s like I can’t believe it when it comes from me.

automatic_thoughts: it’ll happen again
one mistake defines my journey

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
Tends to fail to learn from adverse experience (finding)
Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re holding a very strict view that one mistake defines you. What evidence do you have that this mistake is a defining moment? How might you view this situation if you were talking to a supportive friend? What might a more balanced perspective look like?

patient.query: I guess it’s just hard to shake off those moments when I’ve done poorly. It feels like all anyone sees are those grades, and it gets stuck in my head. If I were talking to a friend, I’d probably say that it doesn’t mean they’re not capable or that they’re a failure. But when it’s me, I just can’t see beyond the current moment. A more balanced view? I... I suppose it would mean remembering that I’m still learning, and one

automatic_thoughts: all anyone sees are those grades
I can’t see beyond the current moment

entailment: Tends to fail to learn from adverse experience (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling stuck in that one snapshot of your performance. What evidence do you have that people only see those grades, and what other aspects of your abilities might they notice? Reminding yourself that learning is a process can help shift the focus from a single moment to the broader journey.

patient.query: Yeah, I guess I do get caught up in that one moment. I just feel like grades are the easiest way for people to judge me, you know? But maybe there are things they notice, like how hard I work or how I help others in group projects. Still, it’s hard to shake the feeling that a bad grade means I’m not good enough overall. It’s tough to shift my focus like that.

automatic_thoughts: grades are the easiest way for people to judge me
a bad grade means I’m not good enough overall
behaviors: getting caught up in the moment

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of pressure that a single grade can define your entire worth. What evidence do you have that a bad grade means you’re not good enough overall? Are there other ways people might see your strengths, like your effort or teamwork? How could you view a low grade as just one piece of information rather than a verdict on your whole self?
